note: All measured on the same Windows/MSVC box. Compiled tiers at -O2/release; CPython for Python. See benchmarks/README.md and benchmarks/crosslang/run_perf.ps1.

workloads.name: count_primes_below(300000)
workloads.unit: ms (whole-program wall time, best-of-9; Python best-of-1)
workloads.note: Lullaby uses the native `%` operator for the trial-division remainder (matching C's `n % d`); the prior `rem(a,b)=a-(a/b)*b` helper measured 1.04x. At parity with C/C++ (drifts 0.99-1.01x run to run).

workloads.name: fib(35) recursive
workloads.note: Now at parity with C. `fib` is purely i64-scalar, so its argument stays in a callee-saved register across both recursive calls (like C at /O2). Two codegen fixes closed the gap: the per-call `if n < 2` compares that register directly (`cmp rbx, 2`, no rax reload), and the recursive arguments `fib(n-1)`/`fib(n-2)` form with a single `lea rcx, [rbx - k]` exactly as C does (was `mov rax,rbx; sub rax,k; mov rcx,rax`). Progression: 1.26x -> 1.09x -> ~1.00x C.

workloads.name: sum 0..1e9 (tight while loop)
workloads.note: The counting-sum loop is now ILP-unrolled: the native backend recognizes `while i < N { acc = acc + i; i = i + 1 }` and folds four iterations per step into one dependent add — `lea rax,[i*4+6]` (the block sum 4*i + 0+1+2+3) then `add acc, rax` — with a scalar remainder. That breaks the serial `acc` dependency chain (one dependent add per FOUR iterations instead of one per iteration), taking native from 0.25 ns/iter (1.96x C) to 0.066 ns/iter — now ~2x FASTER than C (0.52x). Exact under wrapping arithmetic. Interpreter tiers: after slot-based variable resolution the IR and bytecode tiers now run FASTER than the AST tree-walker, which still name-scans.

workloads.name: for-loop reduction (interpreters)
workloads.note: The range `for` interpreters bound the loop variable once and update it in place, instead of re-`define`ing it (allocating a scope and cloning the name `String`) every iteration. Measured ~2x faster across all three interpreter tiers. The native tier compiles `for` to machine code with register-promoted counters and is unaffected. (The while-loop workload above does not exercise this path.)

workloads.name: element-wise map c[i]=a[i]+b[i] (i64 array)
workloads.note: The native backend recognizes `for i: c[i] = a[i] +/- b[i]` over `array<i64>` and emits an SSE2 packed loop (movdqu/paddq/psubq/movdqu, two i64 lanes per iteration) with a scalar tail. Measured against the identical loop forced scalar (two-statement body defeats the matcher). Output is bit-for-bit identical to every interpreter (parity-tested).

workloads.name: bitwise reduction acc = acc ^ a[i] (i64 array)
workloads.note: The native backend recognizes `for i: acc = acc <op> a[i]` for `& | ^` (and `acc += a[i]` for `+`) over `array<i64>` and emits an SSE2 packed loop (`pand`/`por`/`pxor`, seeded with the operator identity, two i64 lanes per iteration, horizontal fold, scalar tail). Measured on XOR against the identical loop forced scalar. Output is bit-for-bit identical to every interpreter (bitwise ops are associative and commutative). Element-wise bitwise maps `c[i] = a[i] <op> b[i]` vectorize the same way.

optimization_history.note: Native backend progression this campaign (fib ns/call vs C, and loop ns/iter vs C).

interpreter_wholeset_vs_ast.stage: borrow-not-clone baseline: ir/bc SLOWER than ast
+slot-based variable resolution (ir/bc reads indexed, not scanned)
+flat bytecode VM (dispatch loop, slot frame) on the bytecode tier
